author: @miketipping1583
text: I think your big red button is the reset button, not the emergency shut off. That box will shut off the power automatically when it reaches the preset over-temperature but you need a way to reset it once that's happened, hence the button and it doesn't need to accessible as you don't want people pressing it until the issue that caused the problem is resolved. I have a coffee machine that has the same system. You need to take the cover off to get to the button, which invalidates the warranty meaning that most people send them back the manufacturer and pay for an engineer to take off one screw and press a button.

author: @1967spark
text:
Is the thermal overload switch wired in series with the element? or are you just using it on the control side?

Be careful when using solid state relays, they almost always fail ON.

If the SSR fails, the timer will make no difference (nor will the stop button). Personally I would have used a contactor to isolate the supply to the SSR (you could still use the timer also if it was needed)

Perhaps 4 core cable would have been better to use to supply the stop/start button, the cable used for immersion heaters although a  bit big would be commonly available and would have a higher temperature rating than most flexes.

author: @EmmittBrownBTTF1
text: The thermostat is not thermo-electric, it uses a working gas and gas law, as gas expands from heating in the probe it increases pressure through the "wire" which is a hollow tube, and expands a metal diaphragm in the control box, this then opens switch contacts per turn on shaft. I see these things in ovens.

author: @mindaugasvaskevicius1818
text: 1) the insulation in the heating element is sand. 2) those old control elements are based on expanding liquid with capillary tube to make them "remote", not bi-metal, not thermocouple. 3)The switch in the back was not a emergency manual cut of, but manual reset thermostat, that is used to indicate that there is something wrong with the unit. 4) NC(stop) button must be in series with the relay contacts. 5) there must be a electro-mechanical cut of for the set, because if it fails short, something should always be there to prevent a complete failure. 6) ssr is preferred to be a zero-crossing type to prevent interference emissions. We implement all of this for electric duct heaters. The biggest one I've designed with embedded control was 180kW(18kw with triacs and then other stages with contactors) without 300kW for multiple step control. For single stage 30kW thyristor based control we used those fancy brick modules, contactor on the supply, and even the heatsinks had there thermal cut offs.

author: @michaelsohocki1573
text: Dear Mr. Murray-Smith, I am a chef and I always had this idea of concentrating solar heat by means of a solar collector like a fresnel lens, into a mirrored funnel, into a mirrored pipe, terminating in the heat region of cooking appliances, like the fryer in your video. We have employed that technology in solar ovens, a popular camping toy, but never really got serious about employing more deeply. I think in a concentration and mirrored pipe, we could send a great deal of heat pretty long way, for almost no cost.

author: @edkoetsier339
text: Hey Rob. A warning.. Those Fotek SSR's are cheap far east rubbish. The traces are just thin copper, and they get hot. If you put 25 amps into a 25 amp Fotek SSR it will get VERY hot and it may burn out, or it may start a fire. They can be used at ~10-12 amps relatively safely. There are also Fotek knock offs, and you can't tell and they are even worse. A knock off of a knock off.. I would advise to go to your local electrical supplier and buy there. Also, you mentioned in your recent bagdad video that you had made a video on how to make carbon felt. I cannot find in members or general. Could you point me to it? Cheers Ed

author: @AndyFletcherX31
text: Please be careful with the rating of the Fotec SSR. Almost all cheap ones on ebay/AliExpress are knockoffs and only use 12A or 16A Triacs regardless of the claimed rating. See BigClive and Great Scott for teardowns.

replies.author: @stevetobias4890
replies.text: Some of the cheap knockoffs are amazingly dangerous. They can potentially cause fires which I doubt the Chinese sellers and manufacturers would take responsibility for even if it was proven that they are at fault.

replies.author: @IrishSkruffles
replies.text: Yeah there's a reason why genuine SSR are far more expensive than eBay ones
